annotations.annotation: Natural Resources Article, §§4-206, 4-220, 4-701, 4-745, 4-1201, and 4-1210; State Government Article, Title 10, Subtitle 2; Annotated Code of Maryland

Regulation .10F, J amended effective March 23, 2020 (47:6 Md. R. 340)

para.text: An individual may apply, on a form prescribed by the Department, for a certificate for emergency epinephrine, which shall be valid for up to 1 year if the applicant:
Is an operator of a youth camp;
Is at least 18 years old; and
Has successfully completed, at the applicant’s expense, an emergency epinephrine educational training program approved by the Department.
The applicant shall submit to the Department with the form required in §A of this regulation a written policy that includes:
Designation of agents;
The name of the approved emergency epinephrine educational training program; and
Procedures to:
Store emergency use epinephrine;
Notify a parent or guardian that emergency use epinephrine is available at camp;
Maintain the emergency use epinephrine in a secure manner;
Report use of emergency use epinephrine according to Regulation .06 of this chapter;
Train an emergency epinephrine certificate holder and agent annually; and
Maintain documentation of an emergency epinephrine certificate holder and agent training for 3 years.
A provider of an emergency epinephrine educational training program may apply to the Department to have the training program recognized as approved for 5 years by submitting:
An application form prescribed by the Department;
A copy of all training program materials, including but not limited to handouts, presentations, and exams; and
The following credentials of the instructor:
Name;
License type as required in §E of this regulation; and
License number.
An emergency epinephrine educational training program shall include:
The signs and symptoms of anaphylaxis;
Use of an emergency use epinephrine pen;
Follow-up procedures with a parent or guardian after an emergency use epinephrine is administered;
A skills demonstration; and
A written examination.
An individual teaching an emergency epinephrine educational training program shall be licensed as a physician, a registered nurse, or a certified nurse practitioner.
The Department shall:
Issue a certificate for emergency epinephrine to an applicant who meets the requirements in §§A and B of this regulation;
Deny, revoke or suspend a certificate for emergency epinephrine from an applicant who does not meet the requirements in §§A and B of this regulation;
Approve an emergency epinephrine educational training program if the training program meets the requirements of §C of this regulation;
Disapprove an emergency epinephrine educational training program if the training program does not meet the requirement of §C of this regulation; and

On or before January 31 of each year, publish a report summarizing the information obtained from the Reportable Incident Report Forms submitted to the Department related to the use of emergency use epinephrine at youth camps.
A physician licensed to practice medicine in the State may prescribe emergency use epinephrine in the name of a certificate for emergency epinephrine holder.
A pharmacist licensed to practice pharmacy in the State or a physician may dispense emergency use epinephrine under a prescription issued to a certificate for emergency epinephrine holder.
A certificate for emergency epinephrine holder may:
On presentment of a certificate for emergency epinephrine, receive from any physician licensed to practice medicine in the State a prescription for emergency use epinephrine; and
Possess and store prescribed emergency use.

In an emergency situation when physician or emergency medical services are not immediately available, a certificate for emergency epinephrine holder or agent may administer emergency use epinephrine to an individual who is experiencing or believed in good faith by the certificate for emergency epinephrine holder or agent to be experiencing anaphylaxis.
Eligibility.

The Commission, in consultation with the Health Services Cost Review Commission and the Maryland Institute for Emergency Medical Services Systems, shall develop a process for the award of grants to Level I, Level II and Level III trauma centers to be used in the delivery of trauma care.
The process developed by the Commission for the award of grants shall include:
Grant applications and review and selection criteria for the award of grants;
Review by the Commission, if necessary, for any project that exceeds Certificate of Need thresholds; and
Any other procedure determined necessary by the Commission.
The Commission may issue grants from any balance carried over to the Fund from prior fiscal years.

The total amount of grants awarded in a fiscal year may not reduce the balance remaining in the Fund at the end of the fiscal year to less than 15 percent of the revenue collected in that fiscal year.

Before awarding grants under this regulation in a fiscal year, the Commission shall report to the Senate Finance Committee and the House Health and Government Operations Committee on the process that the Commission has developed for awarding grants in that fiscal year.

section.heading: Dealer Controlled Electronic Table Games — Requirements.

para.text: Wagering. A facility operator may conduct electronic wagering at a table game in accordance with this chapter.
Electronic wagering at a table game shall be conducted through the use of a dealer controlled electronic table game system.
If a dealer controlled electronic table game system is in use at a table game, a player may make only those wagers that are approved by the Commission.

The computer controlling the dealer controlled electronic table game system shall be under dual key control, with one key controlled by the accounting department and the other key controlled by the table games department or the slot operations department.

All aspects of a dealer controlled electronic table game system, including the computer and any related hardware, software or related devices, shall be tested by an independent certified testing laboratory and approved by the Commission prior to use by a facility operator.
In addition to the requirements for table games under COMAR 36.05, a facility operator using a dealer controlled electronic table game system shall include in its internal controls, at a minimum:
Procedures to ensure the physical security of the computer and related hardware, software and other devices.
Procedures to ensure the integrity and security of all sensitive data and software.
Procedures to ensure that access to sensitive data and software is limited to only appropriate personnel.
Procedures to ensure the logging of the events and the availability of records to enable an effective audit of the conduct of the system and the reporting of revenue.
A dealer controlled electronic table game shall have the ability to authenticate the transmission of data between the various components of the dealer controlled electronic table game system.
A dealer controlled electronic table game system shall:
Credit funds to the game account of a player when a player buys in to a game at a particular table game and debit any remaining funds from the game account when a player cashes out of the game;
Permit a player to:
Wager from a game account;
Collect a losing wager from a game account; and
Pay a winning wager by crediting the amount of the winnings and corresponding wager to the game account;
In the game of Poker:
Debit a game account and increment pot for a wager;
Distribute a winning pot by crediting the game account of a winning player in the appropriate amounts;
Extract the rake from a player or pot according to the rake procedures established under the standard rules for Poker, and debit the game account of a player in the correct amounts; and
Make a player’s balance or table stakes visible to all players in the game;
Depict the transactions described in §F(1), (2) and (3) of this regulation through one or more electronic fund displays that are visible to each player and to the dealer or boxperson;
Disclose to a player at all times the current balance in the player’s game account;
Accurately report and audit the table game’s win or loss, or Poker revenue, in accordance with the standard rules for Poker;
Be capable of generating reports setting forth, by gaming day, for each table game using the dealer controlled electronic table game system:
The total amount deposited into the game account of a player;
The total amount deposited into game accounts by all players;
The total amount credited to the game account of a player in payment of winnings;
The total amount credited to the game accounts of all players in payment of winnings;
The total amount collected from a player as losing wagers;
The total amount collected from all players as losing wagers;
The total amount withdrawn from game accounts by each player;
The total amount withdrawn from game accounts by all players; and
The table game win or loss; and
If applicable, for Poker:
The total amount deducted from the game account of a player for collection of Poker rake time charges under the standard rules for Poker;
The total amount collected from the accounts of each player for collection of Poker rake time charges under the standard rules for Poker;
The total amount collected from Poker pots for collection of Poker rake under the standard rules for Poker; and
The table game Poker revenue.

The display of a virtual chip on a dealer controlled electronic table game that depicts the transactions described in §F(1), (2) and (3) of this regulation is not required to meet the physical requirements for chips under COMAR 36.05.02.
A dealer controlled electronic table game system shall have no more than 28 table game positions in operation for every dealer.
After installation, Agency staff shall inspect a dealer controlled electronic table game system prior to use by a facility operator.
